text.title: Requirements Validation: Process and Quality Attributes
text.summary: Eight quality attributes for requirement evaluation, technique selection rules, session preparation, issue log structure, and sign-off requirements.

rule.statement: Apply validation before development (are we building the right thing?) and verification after implementation (are we building it right?); never conflate the two gates.
rule.rationale: Swapping these gates produces verified implementations of wrong requirements — expensive to fix post-delivery. BABOK v3 ch. 8 and ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 both mandate this sequencing.

rule.statement: Grade every requirement against all eight attributes before declaring it ready for design; a requirement that fails any attribute cannot be baselined.
rule.rationale: Skipping even one attribute creates known defects that surface during design or testing; the cost of fixing a baselined defect is 10-100x the cost of fixing it during validation (Wiegers, Software Requirements, 3rd ed.).

list.item: Correct — accurately represents stakeholder needs (verify against elicitation source, not the spec itself).
Complete — all necessary information is present (no TBD, no placeholder text).
Unambiguous — only one interpretation is possible (test: ask two stakeholders to paraphrase independently).
Consistent — no conflicts with other requirements in the same document or related documents.
Testable — a test can be written to verify completion (if you cannot write a Given/When/Then, the requirement fails).
Traceable — links to its elicitation source and its downstream test case.
Feasible — can be implemented within known constraints (technology, budget, time).
Necessary — delivers business value without gold-plating (nothing beyond the stakeholder's stated need).

rule.statement: Select validation technique based on requirement type: walkthrough for early-stage textual requirements, inspection for critical-system requirements, prototype review for UI/UX requirements, simulation for process requirements.
rule.rationale: A text walkthrough cannot validate a user interface; a formal inspection is overkill for low-criticality narrative requirements. Matching technique to requirement type maximizes detection at minimum cost.

list.item: Walkthrough — informal, author-led, collaborative; best for early-stage, pre-baseline requirements.
Inspection — formal, checklist-driven, defect-focused; best for safety-critical, regulated, or high-risk requirements.
Prototype review — visual and task-based; best for UI/UX requirements where users perform observable tasks.
Simulation — step-through of process scenarios; best for BPMN-modeled process requirements.
User acceptance review — business stakeholder-led; best for final business validation before baseline.

section.title: Session Preparation

rule.statement: Cap any single validation session at 25 requirements; schedule multiple sessions for larger requirement sets rather than extending session length.
rule.rationale: Stakeholder attention drops sharply after 90 minutes; 200 requirements in one session produces rubber-stamp approvals that miss critical defects. Segmentation by functional area maintains focus and accountability.

rule.statement: Name specific individuals for each reviewer role before scheduling; "Product Owner TBD" is not an acceptable agenda entry.
rule.rationale: Sessions without the actual decision-maker are preparation exercises, not validation. Named accountability prevents proxy approvals that lack authority.

section.reference: Validation session agenda with time slots and participant roles

section.title: Issue Log and Sign-off

rule.statement: Classify every issue with severity (H=blocks design, M=needs clarification before implementation, L=wording) and root-cause class (missing info, ambiguous wording, conflict, infeasible, gold-plating) before the session ends.
rule.rationale: Without severity discipline, all issues look equal and re-validation scope cannot be determined; without root-cause class, fix strategies are guesswork.

rule.statement: Require a named human to provide explicit per-AC approve|defer|reject verdict in the sign-off form; bulk approval of a document section is not acceptable.
rule.rationale: Bulk sign-offs are the canonical rubber-stamp failure mode; per-AC verdicts force reviewers to engage with individual requirements and create an auditable record.

section.reference: Sign-off form with per-AC verdict fields and conditions tracking

antipattern.description: Stakeholder signs off without having read the requirements document.
antipattern.reason: Rubber-stamp approval creates a false baseline; defects discovered during design or testing are traced back to an approved requirement, making them expensive change requests rather than cheap pre-baseline fixes.
